stories.headline: Japan Earthquake: 7.7-Magnitude Quake Strikes Off Northeastern Coast, Tsunami Warning Issued Then Downgraded
stories.summary:
A powerful magnitude 7.7 earthquake struck off the northeastern coast of Japan on Monday, prompting authorities to issue a tsunami warning for waves of up to three meters (approximately 10 feet). The Japan Meteorological Agency and the U.S. Geological Survey confirmed the quake, while Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi announced the government had established a crisis management team in response.

The tsunami warning was subsequently downgraded to a tsunami advisory as officials reassessed the threat. Authorities urged residents in coastal areas to stay away from shorelines as a precaution. No immediate reports of casualties or significant structural damage had emerged in the hours following the quake.

Japan's meteorological agency issued an additional warning that a stronger earthquake could strike in the region within the coming week, urging continued vigilance. The country sits along the Pacific Ring of Fire and has extensive tsunami preparedness infrastructure following the devastating 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami.

Four outlets, including Reuters and BBC News, were tracking the story as conditions continued to be assessed.

stories.headline: Update: Trump Says Iran Ceasefire Ends Wednesday, Extension 'Highly Unlikely'; Vance to Depart for Pakistan as Tehran Signals Reluctance
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump stated Monday that the two-week ceasefire with Iran expires "Wednesday evening Washington time" and called a further extension "highly unlikely" if no deal is reached. Speaking in a phone interview, Trump projected confidence that Iran would negotiate while warning of severe consequences if it does not, saying "lots of bombs start going off" should the truce lapse without agreement.

Vice President JD Vance and senior U.S. officials are expected to depart Tuesday for Pakistan ahead of a potential second round of peace talks, but Iran's Foreign Ministry stated that Tehran has no plans to reengage the U.S. "for now," casting doubt on whether negotiations will proceed. Iranian officials have been noncommittal publicly, even as U.S. and allied officials prepare for the Islamabad meeting. GOP Sen. Lindsey Graham was seen arriving at the White House late Monday ahead of the anticipated talks.

Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remained severely reduced, with only 16 ships transiting on Monday as vessel operators remain cautious amid the fragile ceasefire. Trump administration officials, including Energy Secretary Chris Wright, offered mixed signals on gas prices, with Wright telling CNN that prices may not fall below $3 per gallon until next year. A new NBC News poll showed Trump's job approval sinking to a new low amid public concern over rising costs tied to the Iran conflict.

Critics, including Democratic lawmakers and foreign policy analysts, have accused the administration of sending contradictory messages that undermine diplomatic progress. Supporters of Trump's approach argue that military pressure has forced Iran to the table and that a firm deadline is necessary to produce a concrete agreement rather than indefinite stalling.

stories.headline: Russia Has Looted Thousands of Ukrainian Cultural Artifacts During War, Ukraine Says, as Venice Biennale Debate Resurfaces
stories.summary:
Ukraine is renewing international pressure over the systematic looting of its cultural heritage by Russian forces, with officials documenting the removal of thousands of artworks, artifacts, and historical objects from occupied territories since Russia's full-scale invasion in February 2022. The issue has gained fresh urgency ahead of next month's Venice Biennale, which plans to allow Russian representatives to participate for the first time since 2022 — a decision Kyiv says would amount to whitewashing Russian war crimes.

The most documented case involves the Kherson Art Museum, whose director Alina Dotsenko found thousands of artworks missing when Ukrainian forces retook the city in late 2022. Before the war, the museum held more than 14,000 works; Russian forces loaded much of the collection onto trucks as they retreated, and the fate of nearly 10,000 pieces remains unknown. Dotsenko had pre-emptively digitally archived the entire collection, providing prosecutors a detailed record now being used in cooperation with Interpol.

Russia's Culture Ministry did not respond to an Associated Press request for comment. Russian-installed officials in occupied territories previously described the removals as protective measures, and former Kherson deputy administrator Kirill Stremousov, who died before the city's liberation, claimed removed items would eventually be returned.

Ukraine's Culture Minister Tetiana Berezhna has been raising the issue internationally, but documenting losses across the country remains difficult because many institutions lacked the kind of detailed pre-war records that made the Kherson case prosecutable. Cultural property violations can only be pursued in court if items can be identified and proved missing individually.

stories.headline: Tim Cook Stepping Down as Apple CEO After 15 Years; John Ternus to Take Over
stories.summary:
Apple announced Monday that Tim Cook will step down as chief executive officer, ending a nearly 15-year tenure in which he transformed the company into one of the most valuable corporations in the world. Cook, who has led Apple since 2011 following the death of Steve Jobs, will transition to the role of executive chairman effective September 1.

John Ternus, Apple's current head of hardware engineering, will succeed Cook as CEO. Ternus has been a key figure in overseeing the development of Apple's core hardware products and is widely respected within the company's engineering ranks.

Cook's tenure saw Apple's market capitalization soar to historic heights, with major product launches including the Apple Watch, AirPods, and multiple generations of the iPhone. He also oversaw Apple's expansion into services, including Apple TV+, Apple Pay, and the App Store ecosystem, diversifying the company's revenue streams well beyond hardware.

The transition represents one of the most consequential leadership changes in the technology industry in years. Apple is among the most closely watched companies globally, and the handoff from Cook — who himself inherited a seemingly impossible role from Jobs — will be scrutinized by investors, competitors, and the broader business community.

stories.headline: EU Hosts Palestinian Peace Conference as Hungary's Post-Orbán Shift Opens Door to New Israel Policy
stories.summary:
More than 60 nations sent representatives to Brussels on Monday for a European Union-hosted conference on Palestinian stability, security, and long-term peace. The gathering marks a significant move by the EU to assert a more active role in Middle East diplomacy after largely sitting on the sidelines during negotiations that produced the October ceasefire in Gaza.

The conference comes days after Hungarian opposition leader Péter Magyar defeated Prime Minister Viktor Orbán in national elections. Orbán had for years wielded a veto against EU measures critical of Israel, including sanctions on violent Israeli settlers in the West Bank. Magyar has signaled he will not continue that veto policy and may also rejoin the International Criminal Court, which has issued an arrest warrant for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said Monday that with Orbán's veto effectively removed, concrete actions — including targeted sanctions on Israeli settlers — could move forward. Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez, one of the bloc's most vocal critics of Israeli conduct, is expected to formally propose suspending the EU-Israel Association Agreement at a foreign ministers' meeting on Tuesday, though that step is considered unlikely to clear a threshold of support among member states.

The EU has recognized Palestinian statehood in a majority of its 27 member countries and is both the largest provider of aid to Palestinians and Israel's top trading partner, giving it significant economic leverage. While suspension of the trade agreement faces resistance from countries such as Austria and Germany that maintain stronger support for Israel, Monday's conference signals a potential shift in European posture toward the conflict.

stories.headline: DOJ Subpoenas Witnesses Before Grand Jury in John Brennan Investigation
stories.summary:
The Justice Department has subpoenaed several witnesses to testify before a federal grand jury in Washington as part of its ongoing criminal investigation into former CIA Director John Brennan, according to three people familiar with the matter. The subpoenas, issued in recent days, signal prosecutors are pressing forward even after a Florida-based career prosecutor left the case, citing doubts about the legal viability of a potential criminal prosecution.

The investigation centers on allegations that Brennan made false statements before the House Judiciary Committee in 2023 regarding the preparation of the intelligence community's January 2017 assessment, which concluded Russia interfered in the 2016 presidential election on behalf of Donald Trump. The criminal referral was made by Rep. Jim Jordan. Brennan and his legal team have vigorously denied any wrongdoing.

The shift to a Washington grand jury is significant: prosecutors typically bring cases in the jurisdiction where the alleged offense occurred, and Brennan's congressional testimony took place in Washington. A former Justice Department lawyer who supported legal efforts to overturn the 2020 election has been sworn in as a special counselor to the attorney general and is expected to work on the investigation.

The Brennan probe is one of several criminal investigations the Justice Department has opened against Trump's perceived political adversaries since he returned to office. Critics argue the investigations represent politicization of federal law enforcement, while supporters contend they represent legitimate accountability for alleged abuses of intelligence and oversight processes.

stories.headline: California Attorney General Releases Emails Alleging Amazon Colluded With Vendors on Price Fixing
stories.summary:
California Attorney General Rob Bonta on Monday unsealed court documents containing internal Amazon emails that the state alleges show the e-commerce giant coordinating with vendors to raise prices on products sold through competing retailers. The previously redacted communications are central to the state's antitrust lawsuit against Amazon and reveal alleged collusion involving everyday consumer goods including pet treats, khaki pants, and eyedrops.

According to the filing, Amazon employees repeatedly worked with vendors using its platform to pressure competing retailers — including brands such as Levi's and Hanes — into raising their prices, a practice the state says suppressed price competition across the online retail market. California argues this behavior violates antitrust law by allowing Amazon to maintain artificially high prices rather than competing on cost.

Amazon has not yet issued a detailed public response to the newly unsealed materials. The company has previously denied anticompetitive conduct in related legal proceedings, arguing its pricing practices benefit consumers and reflect normal marketplace dynamics.

The case adds to a growing body of regulatory and legal pressure facing Amazon from state and federal authorities. The newly disclosed emails are expected to become a focal point as the litigation advances, with the California attorney general framing them as direct evidence of coordinated price manipulation at scale.

stories.headline: Update: Singer D4vd Charged with First-Degree Murder in Death of 14-Year-Old Girl
stories.summary:
Rising R&B artist D4vd, whose legal name is David Anthony Burke, was charged Monday with first-degree murder and multiple special circumstances in the death of 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez, who disappeared in April 2025 after visiting his Hollywood Hills home. Her dismembered and decomposing remains were discovered in September 2025 in the trunk of a Tesla registered to Burke. Los Angeles County District Attorney Nathan Hochman announced three sets of charges at a press conference: first-degree murder with special circumstances of 'lying in wait,' murder for financial gain, and murder of a witness to an investigation.

Hochman alleged that Rivas Hernandez was a witness in an investigation of sexual acts committed by Burke, and that Burke killed her to protect his musical career. Additional charges include numerous sexual acts with a minor and mutilation of a human body. The DA's office indicated it could eventually seek the death penalty.

Burke, 21, is scheduled to be arraigned Monday in downtown Los Angeles. His attorneys issued a statement saying 'actual evidence in this case will show that David Burke did not murder Celeste Rivas Hernandez and he was not the cause of her death.' LAPD Chief Jim McDonnell defended the length of the investigation, noting that the condition of the remains significantly delayed determining a cause of death and that investigators needed to ensure nothing would jeopardize the case.

The case drew widespread attention given Burke's profile as a breakthrough musical act. The victim's family has not yet spoken publicly. The investigation involved nine major news outlets and represents one of the most high-profile celebrity criminal cases in Los Angeles in recent years.

stories.headline: Update: Tariff Refund Portal Launches as U.S. Importers Begin Filing $166 Billion in Claims
stories.summary:
The Trump administration on Monday launched an online claims system called Cape, allowing U.S. importers and their brokers to begin seeking refunds on more than $166 billion in tariffs that the Supreme Court ruled were imposed without legal authority. The portal's debut marks the first operational phase of the government's effort to comply with court orders requiring reimbursement of tariffs collected under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, plus interest.

The launch represents a materially new development beyond prior coverage of the portal's scheduled opening: the system is now live and actively accepting applications, and officials confirmed the $166 billion figure — higher than the $127 billion cited in earlier reporting — reflecting a broader scope of covered tariffs. Axios reported that the administration framed the launch as a good-faith compliance step while legal and procedural questions about the full repayment process remain unresolved.

The Supreme Court's ruling that Trump lacked constitutional authority to impose the tariffs upended a central pillar of his trade policy. Businesses across manufacturing, retail, and agriculture sectors that paid duties over the past year are expected to file large volumes of claims, though the timeline and mechanics for processing refunds and interest payments have not been fully detailed by the administration.

The rollout is being watched closely by trade groups, importers, and members of Congress from both parties. Supporters of the administration argue the portal demonstrates good-faith compliance with the court; critics contend the process is moving too slowly and that interest owed to businesses continues to accrue.

stories.headline: Update: Oil Prices Surge as Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz Again, U.S. Seizes Iranian Ship
stories.summary:
Global oil prices jumped sharply Monday after President Trump announced the U.S. Navy had intercepted and seized an Iran-flagged cargo ship, adding to market volatility following Iran's decision to close the Strait of Hormuz for the second time in days. Brent crude rose more than 4 percent to $94.20 a barrel, reversing a decline from Friday when Iran had signaled the strait would remain open for the duration of the ceasefire. Iran reversed course on Saturday, announcing the strait would be closed and that any approaching vessels would be targeted, citing the ongoing U.S. naval blockade as a ceasefire violation.

The strait, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas typically flows, has been at the center of a series of rapid reversals since the U.S. and Israel launched military operations against Iran on February 28. Brent crude was trading near $70 a barrel before the conflict and reached nearly $120 on March 9. Analysts noted that markets have grown increasingly skeptical of official statements, with one analyst describing conditions as "physically playing out in real time" on the strait rather than being resolved through diplomacy.

Diplomatic efforts remain uncertain. Trump said U.S. representatives, led by Vice President JD Vance, would be in Pakistan on Monday for negotiations, but Iran's state media said Tehran had "no plans for now to participate" in the talks. The White House has maintained its naval blockade would continue until a deal is reached, a position Iran has said must be reversed before the strait reopens.

Stock markets were mixed in response. U.S. equities fell slightly in early trading, with the Dow Jones down 0.1 percent and the S&P 500 off 0.2 percent. European markets declined more sharply, while Asian indices closed higher earlier in the session. Market analysts warned of continued volatility given the rapidly shifting signals from both governments.

stories.headline: Fed Nominee Kevin Warsh to Pledge Central Bank Independence at Senate Confirmation Hearing
stories.summary:
Kevin Warsh, President Trump's nominee to chair the Federal Reserve, is set to face Senate questioning Monday with his confirmation far from assured. According to prepared testimony, Warsh will pledge to defend the Fed's institutional independence — a notable commitment given that Trump has repeatedly and publicly called for interest rate cuts and has clashed with current Fed leadership.

Warsh's prepared remarks notably do not mention lowering interest rates, departing from the priorities Trump has loudly championed. The 56-year-old former Fed governor has previously criticized the central bank for losing its way and called for a fundamental change in how it operates, language that helped attract Trump's support but has alarmed some senators concerned about central bank autonomy.

The hearing before the Senate Banking Committee comes at a sensitive moment for the Fed, which has been managing monetary policy amid an energy price shock triggered by the Iran conflict and ongoing uncertainty over tariff policy. Some senators from both parties have raised questions about whether Warsh's past statements signal a willingness to subordinate the Fed's decisions to White House preferences.

Warsh's confirmation is considered uncertain, with centrist Republican senators seen as potential swing votes. The outcome will have significant implications for interest rate policy, inflation management, and the Fed's credibility with financial markets at a period of acute global economic stress.

stories.headline: California Coastal Commission Cancels Long-Running Charity Fireworks Show for Nation's 250th Anniversary
stories.summary:
A Fourth of July fireworks tradition in Long Beach, California, that has raised $2 million for local children's programs over 14 years will not take place this year after the California Coastal Commission unanimously upheld a staff decision rejecting a permit for the event. Organizer John Morris had planned an expanded show to mark the nation's 250th Independence Day but says regulators cited environmental concerns despite a decade of testing showing no measurable impact on the bay.

Morris, a 78-year-old local business owner, says he conducted annual water testing before and after the event, deployed underwater cameras to inspect for debris, and submitted eight years of bird surveys — none of which identified any violations or adverse findings. He contends the ruling is inconsistent, noting the commission simultaneously granted SeaWorld a permit for up to 40 nights of fireworks at Mission Bay. A California Coastal Commission spokesman said permits are evaluated case by case.

The decision drew criticism from conservatives, who framed it as an example of California environmental regulation overriding community traditions and charitable activity. The event drew approximately 100,000 attendees annually and was entirely community-funded, including payments to the city for police and fire services. Morris announced he would cancel the July 3 celebration entirely rather than proceed without fireworks.

The story arrives as the country prepares for a series of nationally organized Semiquincentennial celebrations. Critics of the commission's ruling argue the outcome illustrates a tension between local traditions and state regulatory authority, while supporters of the commission say consistent environmental standards must apply regardless of an event's charitable purpose.

stories.headline: More Than 200 Civilians Rescued from IS-Linked ADF Militants in Eastern DR Congo
stories.summary:
Ugandan and Congolese forces conducted a joint military operation against a camp controlled by the Allied Democratic Forces (ADF), an armed group linked to the Islamic State, freeing more than 200 civilians held captive in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo. Children were among those rescued, with the youngest being a 14-year-old girl, according to a statement from the Ugandan military. Several ADF fighters were killed in the assault and a cache of weapons was recovered.

Those freed described harsh conditions in captivity, including forced labor, food deprivation, and punishment for disobedience. Some appeared frail and were suffering from malaria, breathing difficulties, and exhaustion, Ugandan authorities said. Maj. Gen. Stephen Mugerwa, who leads the joint mission, told the freed civilians they were victims of abduction and would be handed over to relevant authorities to reunite with their families.

The ADF, which originated in Uganda in the 1990s, has operated from bases in eastern DR Congo for roughly two decades. Its leader, Musa Seka Baluku, pledged allegiance to the Islamic State in 2016, and the group is responsible for more than half of civilian deaths in the conflict-hit eastern Congo region, according to a 2024 BBC Monitoring study. A 2024 Amnesty International researcher documented kidnappings, killings, and the use of women and girls as sexual slaves occurring with 'alarming frequency.'

Uganda and DR Congo launched a joint offensive against the ADF in 2021, but the operation has so far failed to eliminate the group's capacity for attacks. The ADF has also been blamed for suicide bombings in Uganda's capital, Kampala, in 2021 and attacks on schools in western Uganda in 2023.

stories.headline: Romania's Coalition Government on Verge of Collapse After Largest Party Withdraws Support for Prime Minister
stories.summary:
Romania was thrown into fresh political turmoil on Monday after the largest party in the ruling coalition withdrew its backing for Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan, putting his fragile government on the verge of falling apart. The development marks the latest in a prolonged period of instability for the Eastern European country, which has struggled to maintain stable governance.

The withdrawal of support raises the prospect of another round of political negotiations or elections in Romania, adding uncertainty to a country that is a NATO member and European Union state at a time of broader regional security concerns stemming from the war in Ukraine. Romania has held multiple elections in recent years amid repeated governmental crises.

The political turmoil comes as other Eastern European nations, including Hungary and Bulgaria, have also faced significant political realignments in recent months. Romania's instability has drawn concern from European partners who view steady governance in the region as important to collective Western security commitments.

stories.headline: Artemis II Commander Shares iPhone Video of Earth Setting Behind the Moon, Drawing Millions of Views
stories.summary:
Artemis II mission commander Reid Wiseman shared a video on Monday of Earth setting behind the moon, filmed through a docking hatch window using an iPhone. The clip, which had accumulated more than 12 million views on X, shows the Earth disappearing behind the lunar surface and was described by Wiseman as 'uncropped, uncut with 8x zoom.' Wiseman noted he had 'only one chance in this lifetime' to capture such an image.

Wiseman explained that the iPhone was the ideal instrument for the shot because it was small enough to fit through the docking hatch window. Retired astronaut Chris Hadfield called it the 'coolest video ever taken with an iPhone,' and observers noted it represented the farthest a consumer smartphone had ever been from Earth — roughly 250,000 miles away.

The Artemis II mission, which ended with splashdown on April 10, was the first crewed journey to the vicinity of the moon since Apollo 17 in 1972. The spacecraft carried Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen a record 252,756 miles from Earth, surpassing the distance record set by Apollo 13 in 1970.

NASA is now reviewing data from the Artemis II mission before developing plans for Artemis III, which will also be a lunar flyby without a landing. A potential lunar touchdown is projected for 2028 with Artemis IV, which is intended to establish the foundation for a permanent moon base and serve as a stepping stone toward future Mars missions.

stories.headline: DOJ Demands 865,000 Detroit Ballots, Threatens Court Action if Wayne County Does Not Comply
stories.summary:
The Justice Department has demanded roughly 865,000 ballots and hundreds of thousands of related election records from Wayne County, Michigan — which includes Detroit — stemming from the November 2024 federal election. In an April 14 letter to Wayne County Clerk Cathy Garrett, Assistant Attorney General for Civil Rights Harmeet Dhillon requested all ballots, including absentee and provisional, along with ballot receipts and envelopes, citing federal records-retention law and a stated intent to investigate whether election laws were followed. The department warned that failure to comply within 14 days may result in the United States seeking a court order.

Dhillon cited a 'history of fraud convictions and other allegations' in Wayne County as justification for the demand. The move is part of a broader Trump administration effort to scrutinize election procedures in key swing states, following earlier actions involving 2020 ballots in Georgia and election records in Arizona.

Democrat officials in Michigan sharply rejected the request as politically motivated. Michigan Attorney General Dana Nessel said her office was 'prepared to protect the people's right to vote,' calling the effort a 'circus.' A federal judge has previously rejected a separate Trump administration lawsuit seeking Michigan voter rolls.

Supporters of the investigation argue that ensuring only eligible citizens vote is a legitimate and paramount government duty. Critics contend the demand is an unprecedented intrusion into state election administration with no credible evidentiary basis, and that it is designed to cast doubt on the outcome of a certified election.

stories.headline: U.S. Forces Kill Three in Strike on Alleged Drug Boat in Caribbean
stories.summary:
Three individuals were killed Sunday when U.S. forces struck an alleged narco-trafficking vessel in the Caribbean, according to U.S. Southern Command. The military posted video of the attack and stated the boat was traveling along known drug-trafficking routes. Southern Command did not immediately provide further details on the identities of those aboard or the specific location of the strike.

The operation is part of an ongoing U.S. military effort to interdict drug shipments in the Caribbean and Eastern Pacific under authorities expanded by the Trump administration, which has increasingly directed military assets toward counter-narcotics missions. The use of lethal force against boats on suspected trafficking routes has drawn scrutiny from human rights advocates who question verification procedures before strikes are carried out.

The incident comes amid heightened U.S. military activity in the region and follows a series of counter-narcotics operations in recent months. Officials have not confirmed the nationality of those killed or the type or quantity of drugs, if any, recovered from the vessel.

stories.headline: Sen. Mark Warner Announces Death of 36-Year-Old Daughter After Decades-Long Battle With Juvenile Diabetes
stories.summary:
Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) announced Monday the death of his daughter Madison, age 36, following a lifelong struggle with juvenile diabetes and related health complications. In a joint statement with his wife, Lisa Collis, Warner said the family was 'heartbroken beyond words,' describing Madison as someone who 'filled our lives with love and laughter.'

Madison Warner's death draws attention to the ongoing challenges faced by individuals living with Type 1 diabetes, a condition that can involve serious long-term complications affecting the kidneys, heart, and other organ systems despite advances in insulin therapy and glucose monitoring. Sen. Warner has been a prominent figure in the Senate, serving on the Intelligence and Finance committees, and has previously spoken about his family's personal experiences with chronic illness.

Condolences from colleagues across party lines were expected in the coming days. No details regarding memorial arrangements were immediately announced by the family.

stories.headline: Update: Vance Heads to Pakistan for Iran Ceasefire Talks as Tehran Signals It May Boycott Negotiations
stories.summary:
President Trump announced Monday that a U.S. delegation including Vice President JD Vance, special envoy Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner is en route to Pakistan for a new round of peace talks with Iran, as the current 10-day ceasefire approaches its expiration in roughly 48 hours. Pakistan has moved ahead with preparations in Islamabad, where security is on lockdown, though Iran has publicly stated it has "no plans" for negotiations with the United States, creating significant uncertainty about whether talks will proceed.

Tensions have risen sharply over the weekend after the U.S. Navy seized an Iranian-flagged container ship that attempted to evade the American naval blockade near the Strait of Hormuz — the first such interception since the blockade was imposed. Iran condemned the seizure as an act of piracy, and Chinese President Xi Jinping called for an immediate ceasefire and restoration of normal transit through the strait. Trump said it is "highly unlikely" he would extend the ceasefire if no deal is reached before it expires.

The administration faces divergent pressures heading into the talks. Trump has insisted the Strait of Hormuz will remain blocked until an agreement is finalized, while his energy secretary has cautioned that gas prices may not fall below $3 before 2027. Iran, for its part, has characterized U.S. demands as "maximalist" and a barrier to progress, while continued naval incidents have dimmed prospects for a breakthrough.

The geopolitical stakes are considerable. China has positioned itself as a beneficiary of the standoff without direct military involvement, and energy markets in Asia and Europe are showing signs of stress from restricted transit. Vance's role is under heightened scrutiny after he left the first round of high-level talks without an agreement, and his relationship with Trump and the trajectory of negotiations are being closely watched in both capitals.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Declines to Hear Massachusetts Parents' Challenge to School Gender Transition Policies
stories.summary:
The U.S. Supreme Court on Monday declined to take up an appeal from Massachusetts parents who argued that their school district violated their constitutional rights under the 14th Amendment's due process clause by encouraging their child's social gender transition — including pronoun and name changes — without parental knowledge or consent. The case, Foote v. Ludlow School Committee, was turned away without comment, leaving the lower court ruling in place.

The court's action came despite its having issued an emergency ruling touching on related parental rights questions just last month. The justices had separately turned away a similar parental notification case last December, suggesting the court has so far been reluctant to take up the issue on the merits in a full appellate review, even as the broader landscape of gender-related school policies remains deeply contested across the country.

Supporters of parental notification policies, including many conservative and religious groups, argue that parents have a fundamental constitutional right to direct their children's upbringing and that schools should not be permitted to facilitate significant social changes without parental involvement. Advocates for transgender students and civil liberties organizations counter that such notification requirements can expose vulnerable minors to hostile home environments and undermine their safety and wellbeing.

The Supreme Court's refusal to hear the case does not set a national precedent, and the legal landscape on parental rights in gender transition cases continues to evolve across different federal circuits. A separate and related case — involving Catholic preschools and Colorado's state-funded preschool program — was accepted by the court on the same day, signaling continued interest in adjacent religious and parental rights questions.

stories.headline: FBI Director Kash Patel Sues The Atlantic for $250 Million Over Drinking and Absence Allegations
stories.summary:
FBI Director Kash Patel filed a defamation lawsuit Monday in U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia against The Atlantic and its reporter Sarah Fitzpatrick, seeking $250 million in damages over a story published Friday titled "The FBI Director Is MIA." The article, based on conversations with more than two dozen current and former officials, alleged that Patel had alarmed colleagues with frequent episodes of excessive drinking, conspicuous inebriation, and unexplained absences from his duties leading the nation's top law enforcement agency.

Patel called the claims "false and obviously fabricated" and said he had warned the magazine he would pursue legal action if it published the story. The Atlantic has stood behind its reporting, and the article's author, Sarah Fitzpatrick, is described as a veteran national security journalist. The $250 million figure matches the damages sought in a high-profile defamation case brought by another public figure against a major media outlet in recent years.

The lawsuit adds a new dimension to ongoing scrutiny of Patel's tenure at the FBI, which he has led since being confirmed earlier this year amid considerable Democratic opposition. Critics have argued the bureau has been politicized under his leadership, while supporters contend that Patel has been targeted by a hostile media and entrenched bureaucracy resistant to his reform efforts.

The case will raise foundational questions about defamation standards for public officials, who under long-established Supreme Court precedent must demonstrate "actual malice" — that a publication knew a claim was false or acted with reckless disregard for the truth — to prevail in a libel suit. Media law experts noted the case will be difficult to win at that standard, though it could have a chilling effect on sources willing to speak to reporters about senior government officials.

stories.headline: Rumen Radev's Progressive Bulgaria Wins Landslide in Country's Eighth Election in Five Years
stories.summary:
Former Bulgarian President Rumen Radev's Progressive Bulgaria party won a sweeping victory in Sunday's parliamentary election, capturing 44.7% of the vote and 135 seats in the 240-seat parliament — the first time since 1997 that a single Bulgarian party has secured enough seats to govern alone. The party was founded only four months ago after Radev stepped down from the presidency in January to enter electoral politics directly.

The election was triggered after the previous government attempted to push through a controversial budget in December, sparking mass protests that Radev, then still serving as president, publicly supported. Former Prime Minister Boiko Borisov's GERB party finished second with 13.4%, with the liberal PP-DB coalition third at 12.8%.

Radev, a former MiG-29 fighter pilot and air force commander, is regarded as pragmatic and somewhat pro-Russian. He has criticized EU sanctions against Moscow and called for dialogue with the Kremlin, and he opposes Bulgarian military aid to Ukraine. Analysts compare his likely approach to that of Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico rather than outgoing Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, suggesting he will seek a middle ground rather than outright confrontation with European partners.

Bulgaria is a significant supplier of ammunition and explosives to Ukraine, routed through third countries including neighboring Romania, making Radev's stance on the war closely watched by EU and NATO allies. Despite his majority, Radev will need support from a second party to pass key judicial reform legislation requiring a two-thirds supermajority.

stories.headline: Netanyahu Vows 'Harsh' Discipline After IDF Soldier Filmed Smashing Jesus Statue in Lebanese Christian Village
stories.summary:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu condemned an Israeli Defense Forces soldier who was photographed destroying a statue of Jesus Christ with a sledgehammer in the southern Lebanese Christian village of Debel, promising criminal proceedings and "appropriately harsh disciplinary action." The image showed the soldier smashing a crucifix that formed part of a small shrine in a family's garden on the edge of the village.

Netanyahu said he was "stunned and saddened" by the incident and called it inconsistent with Jewish values. Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar called the soldier's conduct "disgraceful and shameful" and offered an apology to Christians worldwide. U.S. Ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee called for "swift, severe, and public consequences." The IDF stated it is working with the community to restore the statue.

Father Fadi Falfel, a priest in Debel, described the act as a desecration of holy symbols and said the village remains effectively surrounded by Israeli forces despite a U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah announced Thursday. "We thought the ceasefire would bring us some relief but we're still surrounded, unable to travel to and from the town," he said.

Debel has been under Israeli military control since Israel launched operations in southern Lebanon following Hezbollah rocket attacks in support of Iran. The incident drew immediate international attention, particularly given its occurrence on Easter Sunday, and adds diplomatic sensitivity to Israel's ongoing military posture in Lebanon.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Takes Up Case on Religious Exemptions in Colorado State-Funded Preschool Program
stories.summary:
The U.S. Supreme Court agreed Monday to hear arguments in a case brought by a Catholic parish challenging Colorado's exclusion of Catholic entities from a state-funded preschool program. The parish argues it should be exempt from an anti-discrimination provision that Colorado applied to bar its participation in the publicly funded initiative.

The case is the latest in a series of religious rights disputes to reach the high court in recent years and follows earlier rulings that have expanded religious entities' ability to participate in government-funded programs. The court's decision to grant certiorari signals continued interest in defining the boundaries between state anti-discrimination requirements and First Amendment religious freedom protections.

The case arrives amid broader political attention to the court's composition. Senate Republicans, facing a three-seat majority they fear could be at risk in November midterm elections, have quietly noted that a potential retirement by conservative Justice Samuel Alito could energize GOP-leaning voters. Republican senators have been careful not to publicly pressure Alito, but privately view a vacancy as a possible political catalyst.

Democrats and civil liberties groups have raised concerns that a ruling favoring the Catholic parish could further erode state anti-discrimination frameworks by allowing religious institutions to selectively opt out of requirements tied to public funding. Conservatives and religious liberty advocates argue the cases correct what they see as unconstitutional hostility toward religious participation in public programs.

stories.headline: AI Model Claude Mythos Found to Contain Vulnerabilities in Major Operating Systems; Researchers Warn of Cognitive Outsourcing Risks
stories.summary:
Researchers have identified security vulnerabilities in multiple major operating systems connected to Claude Mythos, an artificial intelligence model developed by Anthropic, according to reporting from multiple technology outlets. The discovery adds to growing scrutiny of large language model safety and the systemic risks posed by widely deployed AI systems embedded in critical software infrastructure.

Separately, a new wave of academic and policy research is warning that as large language models take over more cognitive tasks — from health guidance to legal analysis — users may be engaging in a form of "mental outsourcing" that carries underexplored costs to critical thinking and personal decision-making capacity. Studies cited by researchers suggest reliance on AI for complex reasoning tasks may atrophy users' independent analytical skills over time.

On the regulatory and investment front, the United Kingdom's Science, Innovation and Technology Secretary unveiled a £500 million fund this week to boost British AI firms, signaling continued government-level competition with the United States and China for AI leadership. U.S. policymakers have also been debating how to handle dependence on AI platforms deemed too strategically critical for the government to operate without.

Worldcoin's iris-scanning technology, aimed at verifying human identity and blocking fake accounts and scams, also featured in discussions this week as governments and technology companies grapple with identity verification in an era of AI-generated fraud. The convergence of security vulnerabilities, cognitive dependency research, and regulatory investment underscores how rapidly AI policy is moving from abstract debate to concrete governance questions.

stories.headline: Massive 7.5-Magnitude Earthquake Strikes Off Japan's Coast, Tsunami Alert Issued
stories.summary:
A powerful 7.5-magnitude earthquake struck off the coast of Sanriku in northern Japan on Monday afternoon at approximately 4:53 p.m. local time, according to the Japan Meteorological Agency. The quake occurred at a depth of roughly 10 miles below the sea surface, triggering an immediate tsunami alert for the surrounding region.

NHK public television reported that tsunami waves of up to 10 feet could impact the northern coastline in the near term. Residents in coastal areas have been urged to evacuate to higher ground as authorities assess the full extent of the threat.

The region sits along one of the world's most seismically active zones. The Sanriku coast was among the areas hardest hit by the catastrophic 2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami, which killed nearly 20,000 people and triggered the Fukushima nuclear disaster. Authorities and emergency services are on high alert as this is a developing situation.

stories.headline: Democrats Hold Modest Lead on 2026 Congressional Generic Ballot as Redistricting Complicates Outlook
stories.summary:
New polling on the 2026 congressional generic ballot shows Democrats holding a modest but consistent single-digit advantage over Republicans across the majority of recent surveys, according to aggregated data from multiple outlets. Historically, the party out of power tends to gain seats in midterm elections, and early indicators suggest that pattern may be taking shape.

However, analysts caution that redistricting efforts currently underway in several states could significantly alter the number of competitive seats available, meaning the national vote share Democrats need to retake the House remains uncertain. The structural landscape of congressional districts will play a crucial role in translating any popular vote advantage into actual seat gains.

Republican strategists have pointed to the unsettled redistricting picture and historical volatility in polling at this early stage as reasons for caution in interpreting the data. Democrats, for their part, are pointing to the polling leads as evidence of public discontent with the current congressional majority. With the midterms still months away, both parties are closely watching special election results and gubernatorial races for additional signals.

stories.headline: Chinese-American Sculptor Faces Secret Trial in Beijing Over Satirical Mao Artworks
stories.summary:
Gao Zhen, a 69-year-old Chinese-American sculptor known for politically provocative works satirizing Mao Zedong and the Chinese Communist Party, faced a closed-door trial in Beijing last month on charges of "insulting revolutionary heroes and martyrs," a charge carrying a potential three-year prison sentence. Gao, who emigrated to the United States in 2022, was arrested during a family visit to his Beijing studio in mid-2024, at which point authorities seized his artworks and barred his wife and seven-year-old son from leaving China.

The works at the center of the case include pieces created as far back as 2009, such as "Execution of Christ" — which depicts Mao as one of several riflemen in a firing squad — and "Mao's Guilt," a life-sized sculpture of the late leader kneeling in contrition. His brother and artistic collaborator, Gao Qiang, told the BBC the case sends a chilling message: "Even if a work was made 15 years ago, it can still be turned into a crime if today's political climate changes."

China analysts and press freedom advocates describe the prosecution as emblematic of an intensifying crackdown on artistic and political expression under the CCP. Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist Ian Johnson characterized the current moment as "probably the darkest period of time in decades" for freedom of expression in China, noting the crackdown surpasses even the post-Tiananmen period of 1989. The Chinese government has not commented publicly on the trial.

stories.headline: Democrats Win Georgia Special Runoff but Fall Short in Marjorie Taylor Greene's Former District
stories.summary:
Democrat Shawn Harris lost the special runoff election for Georgia's 14th Congressional District — the seat previously held by Marjorie Taylor Greene — but the relatively narrow margin in a deeply Republican district drew attention from both parties. The Associated Press called the race for the Republican candidate, but Harris declared the result a moral victory, telling supporters: "If Democrats, independents and Republicans can do this in a ruby-red district, the Democrats can" compete broadly.

The Georgia race was one of several contests being watched as an early indicator of the 2026 political landscape. Democrats argued the tightened margins in reliably Republican territory reflected voter disillusionment with rising costs, including gasoline and fertilizer prices. A Democratic Party official in Chattooga County, one of Georgia's most conservative, said she had spoken with voters who said they had never previously voted Democratic but were doing so this cycle.

Separately, in Wisconsin, a liberal candidate for the state Supreme Court was projected to win by approximately 17 percentage points — the third consecutive spring Wisconsin Supreme Court election resulting in a liberal victory. Analysts noted that Democratic voters have shown sustained turnout advantages in off-cycle elections, consolidating a liberal majority on the court.

Republicans pointed to the Georgia seat being retained as confirmation of their structural advantage in rural and exurban districts, while Democrats argued the national environment — driven by economic concerns tied to the Trump administration — was shifting voters in their direction ahead of the midterms.

stories.headline: Update: U.S.-Iran Ceasefire Takes Effect; 10-Day Truce Holds as Diplomacy Intensifies
stories.summary:
A 10-day ceasefire between the United States and Iran is now in effect, with Iran-backed Hezbollah voicing support for the truce as negotiations between the two countries continue. The halt to weeks of fighting is being marked cautiously by diplomats and regional observers, with the BBC's chief international correspondent reporting from Iran that diplomatic efforts to prevent a return to war are intensifying but remain fragile.

The ceasefire follows weeks of escalating conflict that included the U.S. Navy's interception of Iranian cargo vessels in the Strait of Hormuz and an attack on the Iranian ship Iris Dena, which killed 104 people. President Trump has confirmed U.S. forces "blew a hole" in the vessel's engine room and released footage of the strike. Survivors of the Iris Dena were among personnel repatriated as part of early diplomatic gestures.

Iranian officials have signaled they want a resolution to the long-running hostility with the United States but are unwilling to accept what they describe as maximalist American demands. BBC reporting noted that 21 hours of direct talks were insufficient to resolve 47 years of antagonism, and questions remain about whether the truce can hold.

A separate development in Lebanon saw a ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah enter its second day, with BBC correspondents reporting from the border town of Khiam. President Macron blamed a recent attack in the region on Hezbollah, which denied any connection. More than 1,400 buildings have been destroyed in Lebanon since early March, according to BBC Verify analysis.

stories.headline: New Zealand Declares State of Emergency in Wellington After Record Flash Flooding
stories.summary:
New Zealand declared a state of emergency in its capital city Wellington on Monday after torrential rain caused severe flash flooding across the North Island. Wellington received a record 77 millimeters of rain in under an hour, according to Mayor Andrew Little, submerging vehicles, uprooting trees, and triggering landslides that struck residential neighborhoods.

Authorities advised residents to remain indoors, with rain forecast to continue for the next day and a half. More than a dozen people have been evacuated, several schools have closed, and some flights at Wellington Airport have been cancelled. A 60-year-old man in the Karori suburb was reported missing, though no fatalities have been confirmed as of Monday.

Minister for Emergency Management Mark Mitchell warned that the worst of the weather was expected Monday evening and urged residents in low-lying or flood-prone areas to relocate to family or friends as a precaution. The Wellington City Mission has been opened as a shelter for displaced residents.

The flooding comes less than a week after Cyclone Vaianu swept through the North Island, compounding damage to infrastructure and communities still recovering. Scientists have noted that climate change has increased both the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events globally.

stories.headline: DNA and Legal Process Identifies British Soldier Fathers of Dozens of Children Born Near Kenya Army Base
stories.summary:
A BBC World Service investigation has revealed that a ground-breaking DNA and legal process has identified 20 British military personnel — including soldiers and contractors — as the fathers of children born near a British Army base in Kenya. Paternity has been legally confirmed in 12 cases by a UK Family Court judge, with most of those children now eligible to register for British citizenship and, where applicable, child support payments.

The British Army Training Unit Kenya (Batuk), established in 1964 in Nanyuki, sees more than 5,000 British personnel pass through annually. UK solicitor James Netto and Kenyan lawyer Kelvin Kubai say they have documented nearly 100 such cases and believe many more exist. The process used commercial genealogy databases containing nearly 30 million genetic profiles to cross-reference DNA samples collected in Kenya.

A two-year Kenyan parliamentary inquiry published last December accused British soldiers of operating within "a culture of impunity" at the base, citing sexual abuse, two allegations of murder, rights violations, environmental destruction, and the abandonment of local children. The UK Ministry of Defence said it "deeply regrets those issues and challenges" arising from its defence presence in Kenya and stated it continues to take action to address them.

The affected children range in age from three to 70 years old. Some were told their fathers had died; others did not know their fathers' identities. Many have faced financial hardship and, in some cases, family ostracism.

stories.headline: Senate Republicans Eye Potential Alito Retirement as Possible Midterm Boost
stories.summary:
Senate Republicans concerned about their three-seat majority heading into the 2026 midterms are privately discussing the potential retirement of Supreme Court Justice Samuel Alito as a development that could energize conservative voters, according to reporting from The Hill. GOP senators are being careful not to publicly pressure Alito, a leading conservative voice on the court, but view a vacancy as an "October surprise" that could shift the political dynamic in their favor.

The prospect reflects broader Republican anxiety about the midterm environment. Recent generic ballot polling shows Democrats with a modest advantage in congressional races, consistent with historical patterns in which the party out of power gains ground during midterm cycles. Democrats lead in the majority of recent surveys, though by single-digit margins.

Republicans argue that a Supreme Court vacancy would rally their base by highlighting the long-term stakes of Senate control, a dynamic that has historically driven conservative turnout. Democrats have countered that court-related messaging has become less effective for Republicans following the overturning of Roe v. Wade, and that economic concerns — including tariffs and cost-of-living pressures — are dominating voter priorities.

Redistricting efforts currently underway in several states add another variable to the 2026 landscape, with analysts noting that the national vote share Democrats would need to retake the House depends significantly on the outcome of those map-drawing processes.

stories.headline: Update: Tariff Refund Portal Opens Monday for U.S. Importers Seeking $127 Billion in Repayments
stories.summary:
U.S. Customs and Border Protection is set to launch an online refund portal at 8 a.m. Monday, allowing importers and their brokers to begin claiming refunds on tariffs the Supreme Court ruled President Trump imposed without constitutional authority. The court's 6-3 February ruling found Trump exceeded his powers when he cited the U.S. trade deficit as a national emergency to set new import tax rates on goods from nearly every country under a 1977 emergency powers law. A subsequent ruling by the U.S. Court of International Trade determined affected companies were entitled to reimbursement.

As of April 14, more than 56,000 importers had completed registration with CBP's electronic payment system, making them eligible for refunds totaling approximately $127 billion including interest. The agency said over 330,000 importers collectively paid roughly $166 billion on more than 53 million shipments, though not all qualify for the initial phase of the rollout, which is limited to tariffs that were estimated but not finalized or within 80 days of a final accounting.

Once CBP approves a claim, refunds are expected to take 60 to 90 days to be issued, and the government plans to process them in phases, starting with more recent payments. Legal experts advising importers are urging clients to carefully document all relevant forms submitted to CBP, warning that a single ineligible entry could cause an entire claim to be rejected.

Any refunds that trickle down to consumers who were billed for tariff costs would likely arrive slowly given the layered process. Attorneys familiar with the portal caution that technical issues are possible at launch given the high volume of expected activity.

stories.headline: India's $26 Billion Metro Expansion Falls Far Short of Ridership Projections
stories.summary:
India has invested more than $26 billion since 2014 to expand its metro rail network from under 300 kilometers to more than 1,000 kilometers across nearly two dozen cities, but most systems are carrying a fraction of the passengers planners projected. A 2023 Indian Institute of Technology Delhi report found ridership running at just 25 to 35 percent of projected figures across corridors, a figure experts say has not significantly improved since. In some tier-3 cities such as Kanpur, actual ridership was as low as 2 percent of estimates.

Mumbai's new Aqua Line, a fully underground corridor that opened last year and was projected to carry nearly 1.5 million passengers daily, is currently seeing about a tenth of that figure. Commuters and station staff cite high ticket prices as a primary deterrent in a city where other public transit options, while crowded, are significantly cheaper. Only Delhi's metro network, India's largest, has approached its ridership targets — though transport experts say that is partly a result of the system counting interchanges as separate trips.

Experts point to a confluence of factors behind the shortfall, including consultants overstating projected demand to secure project approvals, poor last-mile connectivity, and fares set too high for the majority of urban commuters. Cities that built metro lines without integrating them into broader transit networks have seen particularly poor results.

The underperformance raises questions about the return on investment for one of the Modi government's signature infrastructure initiatives, and analysts say future expansion plans may need to be reassessed in light of actual usage data.
